title: Iʿrab of the Present-Tense Verb (Al-Fiʿl al-Mudariʿ)

sections.text: Iʿrab of the Present-Tense Verb

The present-tense verb is the only verb that is muʿrab (declinable) among the verbs — its ending changes according to the operators that enter upon it. It has three iʿrab states: nominative (rafʿ), accusative (nasb), and jussive (jazm).

The present-tense verb is in the nominative if not preceded by an accusative- or jussive-governing particle; in the accusative if preceded by one of the accusative-governing particles; and in the jussive if preceded by one of the jussive-governing particles.
First: Putting the Present-Tense Verb in the Nominative
The present-tense verb is in the nominative when free of accusative- or jussive-governors — i.e., not preceded by any nasb- or jazm-governing particle.
Markers of the Nominative for the Present-Tense Verb

items: Manifest damma: when ending in a sound consonant, e.g., yaktubu, yadrusu.
Estimated damma: when ending in a weak letter (alif, waw, or yaʾ), e.g., yasʿa, yadʿu, yaqdi.
Retention of the nun: in the Five Verbs (the present tense joined to alif al-ithnayn, waw al-jamaʿa, or yaʾ al-mukhataba), e.g., yaktubani, yaktubuna, taktubina.

items.text: — The student strives in his lessons.
— The believer strives toward goodness.
— The preacher calls to Allah.
— The judge rules with truth.
— The two students write the lesson.
— The students write the lesson.
— You (fem.) write the lesson.

sections.text: Al-afʿal al-khamsa (the Five Verbs) are every present-tense verb attached to alif al-ithnayn, waw al-jamaʿa, or yaʾ al-mukhataba. Their patterns are: yafʿalani, tafʿalani, yafʿaluna, tafʿaluna, tafʿalina.
Second: Putting the Present-Tense Verb in the Accusative

The present-tense verb is put in the accusative when preceded by one of the accusative-governing particles, namely: an, lan, kay, lam al-taʿlil, hatta, faʾ al-sababiyya, waw al-maʿiyya, lam al-juhud, idhan.
Particles That Put the Present-Tense Verb in the Accusative
An: an infinitival, accusative-governing, future-pointing particle. The verb following it is interpreted together with it as a verbal noun (masdar muʾawwal).

items.text: — It pleases me that you should succeed.
— I love to read the Quran.

sections.text: Lan: an accusative-governing, negating, future-pointing particle, indicating the negation of an action in the future.

items.text: — I will never be late for attendance.
— The lazy one will never win.

sections.text: Kay: an infinitival, accusative-governing particle conveying the meaning of purpose ('in order to').

items.text: — Strive in order to succeed.
— Study in order to excel.

sections.text: Lam al-taʿlil (the lam of purpose): a kasra-bearing lam that enters upon the present-tense verb to convey purpose; it puts the verb in the accusative by an obligatorily implicit 'an'.

items.text: — I came in order to learn.
— I went to the mosque in order to pray.

sections.text: Hatta: a preposition / accusative-governing particle conveying the meaning of an end-point or purpose; it puts the present-tense verb in the accusative by an obligatorily implicit 'an'.

items.text: — Strive until you succeed.
— I walked until I reached the city.

sections.text: Faʾ al-sababiyya (the faʾ of causation): a faʾ indicating that what precedes it is a cause for what follows. It is preceded by a negation or a request (command, prohibition, interrogative, wish, hope, offer, exhortation).

items.text: — Strive — so that you may succeed.
— Do not be lazy — lest you regret.
— Will you visit us so that we may honor you?

sections.text: Waw al-maʿiyya (the waw of accompaniment): a waw indicating accompaniment, preceded by a negation or a request, like faʾ al-sababiyya.

items.transliteration: la tanha ʿan khuluqin wa-taʾtiy-a mithl-a-hu
items.text: — Do not forbid a trait while doing the like of it.
— Do not eat fish while drinking milk.

sections.text: Lam al-juhud (the lam of denial): a kasra-bearing lam preceded by a negated form of 'kana' (ma kana, lam yakun); it puts the present-tense verb in the accusative by an obligatorily implicit 'an'.

items.text: — Allah was not to punish them.
— The believer would never lie.

sections.text: Idhan: a particle of response, recompense, and accusative-governance — indicating a response and recompense to prior speech. It puts the present-tense verb in the accusative under conditions: it must be at the head of the speech, the verb must indicate the future, and nothing may separate it from the verb other than an oath.

items.text: — ('I will visit you tomorrow.') — Then I shall honor you.
— ('I will strive.') — Then you shall succeed.

sections.text: Markers of the Accusative for the Present-Tense Verb

items: Manifest fatha: if ending in a sound consonant or in a weak waw or yaʾ, e.g., lan yaktub-a, lan yadʿuw-a, lan yaqdiy-a.
Estimated fatha: if ending in alif, e.g., lan yasʿa.
Dropping of the nun: if it is one of the Five Verbs, e.g., lan yaktub-a, lan yaktub-u, lan taktub-i.

items.text: — The student will not neglect his assignment.
— The lazy one will never strive toward glory.
— The believer will never call upon any but Allah.
— The unjust will never rule with justice.
— The two students will not be late.
— The students will not be late.
— You (fem.) will not be late.

sections.text: Third: Putting the Present-Tense Verb in the Jussive
The present-tense verb is put in the jussive when preceded by one of the jussive-governing particles. These are of two kinds: those that govern a single verb, and those that govern two verbs.
First: Particles Governing a Single Verb

items: Lam: a particle of negation, jussive-governance, and tense-shift; it negates the action in the past.
Lamma: a particle of negation, jussive-governance, and tense-shift; it negates the action in the past while extending the negation up to the moment of speaking.
Lam al-amr (the imperative lam): a kasra-bearing lam indicating a request.
La al-nahiya (the prohibitive 'la'): a particle of prohibition and jussive-governance, indicating a request to refrain from an action.

items.text: — The student did not attend.
— The teacher has not yet come.
— Let every student strive.
— Do not neglect your assignment.

sections.text: Difference between 'lam' and 'lamma': 'lam' negates the action in the past, and its negation may end or continue. 'Lamma' negates the action in the past with the negation continuing up to the moment of speaking, and with the expectation that it may yet occur.
Second: Particles Governing Two Verbs

These are the conditional jussive-governing particles. They put two verbs in the jussive: the first is the conditional verb (fiʿl al-shart), and the second is the response of the condition (jawab al-shart). They are: in, man, ma, mahma, mata, ayyana, ayna, anna, haythuma, kayfama, ayyu.

rows: Per what it is annexed to

items.text: — If you strive, you will succeed.
— Whoever sows reaps.
— Whatever good you do, you will find it [waiting].
— Whatever you conceal, Allah knows it.
— Whenever you come to us, you will find us.
— Wherever you go, I shall go with you.
— However you are, your companion will be.
— Whichever book you read, you will benefit from it.

sections.text: Markers of the Jussive for the Present-Tense Verb

items: Sukun: if ending in a sound consonant, e.g., lam yaktub.
Dropping of the weak letter: if ending in alif, waw, or yaʾ, e.g., lam yasʿ, lam yadʿu, lam yaqdi.
Dropping of the nun: if it is one of the Five Verbs, e.g., lam yaktuba, lam yaktubu, lam taktubi.

items.text: — The student did not neglect his assignment.
— The lazy one did not strive toward glory.
— The believer did not call upon any but Allah.
— The judge did not rule with injustice.
— The two students were not late.
— The students were not late.
— You (fem.) were not late.

sections.text: The Response of the Condition (Jawab al-Shart) and Its Connection to Faʾ

Jawab al-shart must be connected to a faʾ in seven cases — gathered in the poet's verse: 'A nominal sentence, an imperative, an inflexible verb / and with ma, qad, lan, and the future markers'. They are: when jawab al-shart is a nominal clause; an imperative clause (command, prohibition, interrogative); an inflexible verb; or a verb preceded by ma, qad, lan, sa-, or sawfa.

items.text: — If you strive, you are a successful one. (nominal clause)
— If you have succeeded, then strive in giving thanks. (request)
— Whoever was lazy — how excellent is what he deserves [ironic]. (inflexible verb)
— If you have done wrong, you are no doer of good. (preceded by ma)
— If you strive, you have already won. (preceded by qad)
— If you are lazy, you will never succeed. (preceded by lan)
— If you study, you will win. (preceded by sa-)

sections.text: Why must jawab al-shart be connected to faʾ in these cases?

Because in these cases the response of the condition could not naturally serve as a condition. Therefore the link of recompense between it and the conditional verb is established only via the faʾ, which conveys causation and sequence.
Iʿrab Table for the Present-Tense Verb

rows: Manifest or estimated damma; retention of the nun in the Five Verbs
Manifest or estimated fatha; dropping of the nun in the Five Verbs

rows.transliteration: lan yaktuba, lan yasʿa, lan yaktubu

rows: Sukun; dropping of the weak letter; dropping of the nun in the Five Verbs

rows.transliteration: lam yaktub, lam yasʿ, lam yaktubu

sections.text: One must distinguish between the original (asliyya) and substitute (farʿiyya) iʿrab markers. The damma, fatha, and sukun are the original markers, while the retention of the nun, the dropping of the nun, and the dropping of the weak letter are substitute markers that stand in their place.

The present-tense verb becomes mabni in only two cases (and is not muʿrab in those): mabni on sukun if nun al-niswa is attached, and mabni on fatha if nun al-tawkid is directly attached. In all other cases it is always muʿrab.

items.text: — The female students write the lesson. (mabni on sukun)
— By Allah, I shall surely strive. (mabni on fatha)

sections.text: Allah Most High said: "And do not approach unlawful sexual intercourse. Indeed, it is ever an immorality and is evil as a way." — Quran 17:32 (Sahih International)

In His saying 'la taqrabu' (لا تَقْرَبُوا), 'taqrabu' is a present-tense verb in the jussive by 'la al-nahiya', its sign of jazm being the dropping of the nun (because it is one of the Five Verbs). Waw al-jamaʿa is the subject (faʿil).
